items.title: FPL 2026/27 IS HERE! | MY TEAM for Fantasy Premier League New Season Gameweek 1
items.headline: Bruno Fernandes and Haaland anchor an aggressive Gameweek 4 wildcard draft
items.summary: Up front, Erling Haaland remains close to essential because Bournemouth, Crystal Palace and Coventry make him the standout captaincy safety net, especially in Gameweek 3 when alternatives look thin. João Pedro is the second forward at £7.5m, valued for last season’s consistency in a weak forward pool and his point-to-prove narrative after missing Brazil’s summer squad, though Chelsea investment may be better from Gameweek 4 onward. The bench is purely structural: Phillips is a non-playing £4.0m goalkeeper, Gomes is the preferred £5.0m midfielder with a useful Gameweek 3 home fixture against Leeds, Van Ewijk is a £4.0m attacking Coventry right-back who covers Neco Williams in Gameweek 2 against Hull, and Walle Egeli is the best £4.5m forward bench body because he at least has a path to occasional Ipswich minutes.
key_points: Plan is to attack the first three gameweeks and wildcard in Gameweek 4, using early information on rotation, World Cup fatigue, new systems and defensive contribution points.
Lammens is picked over £4.5m goalkeepers because Manchester United face Hull, Ipswich and Everton before the planned wildcard.
Captaincy is between Bruno Fernandes and Erling Haaland for the opening weeks, with Bruno currently favoured for Gameweek 1 against Hull and Haaland vice-captain.
Manchester United double-up in midfield: Bruno Fernandes plus Bryan Mbeumo, who benefits from strong fixtures and limited right-wing competition.
Defensive core includes Virgil van Dijk for Liverpool’s clean sheets, set-piece threat and DefCon upside, plus Mosquera as a cheap Arsenal route while Saliba is injured.
Neco Williams is backed as a potential new Munoz under Oliver Glasner at Nottingham Forest, playing far higher than Ola Aina in preseason.
Haaland is treated as near-essential because Bournemouth, Crystal Palace and Coventry give him prime captaincy appeal, especially in Gameweek 3.
Bench structure targets cheap cover: Gomes for Gameweek 3, Van Ewijk for Gameweek 2 versus Hull, and Walle Egeli as a £4.5m forward who may actually get minutes.

items.title: My First Draft 2026/27 #fpl #fantasypremierleague #fpltips
items.headline: Haaland anchors a budget-first opening FPL draft after a 588th-place finish
items.summary: Defence is also deliberately lean. Rodon is backed as a £4.5m value pick, while Neco Williams is treated as a possible attacking bargain if he can become Nottingham Forest’s version of Daniel Muñoz under Glasner. Mosquera is the more conditional selection: at £5.5m he could be excellent value while Saliba is sidelined, but that depends on Arsenal not signing another right-sided centre-back.
key_points: Kinsky is the budget goalkeeper pick at £4.5m, with value tied to expected improvement under De Zerbi.
Rodon and Neco Williams keep the defence cheap, with Williams viewed as a potential attacking wing-back bargain.
Mosquera is selected as a £5.5m opportunity while Saliba is out, but Arsenal transfer activity could change that.
Fernandes is close to locked in because of strong early fixtures: Hull away in Gameweek 1 and Ipswich at home in Gameweek 2.
Cole Palmer at £9.5m is the most likely downgrade if funds are needed, though a summer off and Alonso’s arrival offer upside.
Liverpool’s penalties are a key watch after Salah’s exit, with Ekitike likely but Szoboszlai also possible.
Sarr is backed to remain productive despite a managerial change, while Ndiaye appeals as a cheaper Everton penalty taker.
Haaland is still considered very difficult to omit even at £15.5m, despite several attractive £6m forwards.

items.title: MY FIRST FPL 2026/27 TEAM 🔥 BEST RANK 3K! 🌍
items.headline: Palmer-led triple Chelsea attack anchors a high-risk opening FPL draft
items.summary:
The first 2026/27 FPL draft leans into midfield spend and an aggressive triple Chelsea attack, shaped by several rule and pricing changes. Price changes now begin after Gameweek 1, official live rank, league and points tools are added, and bonus-point tweaks appear to help goalkeepers, full-backs and attacking midfielders in particular. Defensive contributions remain a major consideration, with defenders needing 10 actions and midfielders 12 for a two-point reward.

In goal, Verbruggen is preferred at 4.5m because Brighton rank fifth for projected clean sheets over the first six gameweeks, behind Arsenal, Manchester City, Manchester United and Everton, while Raya’s 6.0m price is seen as too expensive despite Arsenal’s elite clean-sheet outlook. The defence is Guehi, Jac and Mosquera, with some caution around World Cup finalists because mandatory three-week rests may affect Gameweek 1 availability. Mosquera is highlighted as a major value route into Arsenal at 5.5m, 2.5m cheaper than Gabriel, if he starts at right centre-back while Saliba is sidelined. Jac is viewed as another strong value pick at 5.0m if nailed in Liverpool’s defence under Iraola, especially compared with Van Dijk at 6.5m.

Midfield is where most of the money goes: Mbeumo, Rogers, Palmer, Szoboszlai and Anderson. Mbeumo is chosen over Bruno Fernandes for Manchester United’s Gameweek 1 trip to Hull, with Hull targeted after promotion via sixth place in the Championship and weak underlying numbers. Mbeumo is also 4.0m cheaper than Bruno and compares favourably with Cunha at the same price after posting a much stronger expected goal involvement figure last season, 17.2 versus 10.3. Szoboszlai is considered underpriced at 7.0m because he has several routes to points — set pieces, possible penalties, corners and some defensive contributions — while Anderson remains attractive at 6.5m after moving to Manchester City because his defensive contribution output was league-leading at Forest and may translate in a Rodri-like role.

The boldest call is triple Chelsea attack, with Palmer, Rogers and Joao Pedro joined by Haaland up front in a 3-5-2. Chelsea are backed to resemble last season’s Manchester United: no Europe, a strong squad, and a top manager in Xabi Alonso. Joao Pedro’s previous season is rated highly, Palmer is expected to benefit from a full summer off after injury issues, and Rogers is accepted as streaky but potentially boosted by better teammates. The key upside spot is Gameweek 4, when Chelsea host Hull and Palmer becomes an obvious captain option, while Haaland faces Manchester United away despite his strong derby record. Haaland’s 15.5m price is accepted as expensive but understandable given the lack of other premium anchors, with Salah gone, Saka set to miss the start and Palmer still carrying some uncertainty.
key_points: Opening draft lines up in a 3-5-2: Verbruggen; Guehi, Jac, Mosquera; Mbeumo, Rogers, Palmer, Szoboszlai, Anderson; Joao Pedro, Haaland.
Mbeumo is preferred to Bruno Fernandes for Manchester United coverage, mainly due to the 4.0m saving and better recent xGI than Cunha at the same price.
Triple Chelsea attack is the main differential play, built around Palmer, Rogers and Joao Pedro with Chelsea backed due to no Europe, a stronger squad and Xabi Alonso.
Palmer is the standout Gameweek 4 captaincy idea at home to Hull, effectively doubling down on the Chelsea attacking punt.
Mosquera is flagged as a potential value Arsenal defender at 5.5m if he starts while Saliba is out, but new centre-back signings could threaten him.
Jac is a 5.0m Liverpool defensive value pick if nailed, especially with Van Dijk priced at 6.5m.
Szoboszlai at 7.0m is viewed as underpriced because of set pieces, possible penalties, corners and defensive contribution potential.
World Cup rest rules create Gameweek 1 availability risk for finalists, so preseason minutes and training returns need monitoring.

items.title: SAM'S FIRST FPL DRAFT! ✅ | BEST RANK 3,499 🔥 | FPL Tips 26/27
items.headline: Haaland and Bruno anchor flexible first FPL draft built around early fixture swings
items.summary:
The first draft leans heavily on the opening fixture ticker, with Liverpool, Manchester United and Manchester City driving most of the early thinking before the first international break. The Gameweek 1 deadline is 21 August, leaving more time than usual for pre-season evidence and late transfer-window news, but the core idea is already clear: build around strong fixtures, preserve price-point flexibility and keep £0.5m in the bank for early moves when prices start shifting.

Erling Haaland and Bruno Fernandes are the premium locks despite their prices. Haaland offers standout captaincy appeal with Manchester City hosting Bournemouth, Coventry and Sunderland in the first five gameweeks, while Bruno is particularly attractive for Manchester United’s opening run, including Hull and Ipswich in the first two. Bruno looks a strong Gameweek 2 captain, and owning both gives captaincy coverage without needing an immediate restructure. Gabriel is initially considered as the premium defender, but the compromises elsewhere are too severe, so the money is spread through cheaper defensive routes instead.

The draft squad is Kinsky in goal, with Van Hecke, Mosquera and Liverpool’s Jacquet in defence; Bruno Fernandes, Dominik Szoboszlai, Morgan Rogers and Matheus Fernandes in midfield; and Haaland, Benjamin Sesko and Jean-Philippe Mateta up front. Rushworth, Tyrick Mitchell, Grimes and Giles make up the bench, with £0.5m spare. Kinsky is paired with Rushworth in the hope that Rushworth completes a move back to Coventry, where he excelled in the promotion season, because Coventry rotate well with Spurs. If that move does not happen, Manchester United’s Lammens is a possible alternative goalkeeper route.

Defence is deliberately value-led. Van Hecke is preferred to Pedro Porro for now because he is cheaper and may offer defensive contribution points plus set-piece threat, though the Spurs defensive double-up is acknowledged as risky. Mosquera is the current Arsenal route, but a cheaper Arsenal centre-back signing such as Konsa would change the structure immediately. Jacquet is picked as the cut-price way into Liverpool’s defence, though Kerkez remains tempting if pre-season suggests he is nailed. In midfield, Szoboszlai looks underpriced at £7.0m given Liverpool’s fixtures and set-piece involvement, while Rogers offers a strong price point even if he can be frustrating to own. Matheus Fernandes is a cheap Spurs punt after an attacking pre-season outing, although James Maddison is an alternative if fitness and captaincy rumours point to secure starts.

Up front, Mateta is viewed as strong value at £6.5m with good Crystal Palace fixtures and a likely starting role under the new manager. Sesko is the main risk-reward call: if pre-season suggests he leads the line for Manchester United, the double-up with Bruno could be powerful before European rotation begins. If not, the structure may switch to João Pedro up front with another United midfielder such as Cunha or Mbeumo instead of Rogers.
key_points: Current draft: Kinsky; Van Hecke, Mosquera, Jacquet; Bruno Fernandes, Szoboszlai, Morgan Rogers, Matheus Fernandes; Haaland, Sesko, Mateta. Bench: Rushworth, Mitchell, Grimes, Giles, with £0.5m in the bank.
Premium core is Haaland plus Bruno Fernandes, mainly for captaincy coverage across Manchester City and Manchester United’s strong first five fixtures.
Bruno Fernandes is highlighted as a strong Gameweek 2 captain option, while Haaland has major home fixtures against Bournemouth, Coventry and Sunderland in the opening five.
Rushworth is only viable if he moves back to Coventry; otherwise the goalkeeper setup may shift, potentially towards Lammens at Manchester United.
Szoboszlai is seen as a potentially underpriced £7.0m Liverpool midfielder with good fixtures and some set-piece appeal, though penalties are not expected.
Sesko is a pre-season watch: if he starts regularly for Manchester United, he becomes an exciting early differential; if not, João Pedro plus another United midfielder is the likely alternative.
Defence is built for value, with Jacquet as the cheaper Liverpool route, Mosquera as an Arsenal placeholder, and Tyrick Mitchell preferred to the more expensive Daniel Munoz at Crystal Palace.
The draft prioritises flexible price points and keeps £0.5m spare to react quickly to early price changes, unexpected starters and late transfer-window developments.

items.title: FPL 2026-27 IS GOING LIVE! | Fantasy Premier League Launch Party
MY FPL FIRST DRAFT! 📱 FPL IS LIVE 🚨 | Fantasy Premier League Tips 2026/27
items.headline: Haaland and Bruno headline first 26-27 FPL draft as Gabriel is priced out
items.summary:
The first draft for the 2026-27 Fantasy Premier League season comes in at exactly £100.0m, built around Erling Haaland and Bruno Fernandes but deliberately swerving £8.0m Gabriel. The early view is that FPL has priced the biggest premiums fairly, yet left enough value elsewhere to build a strong squad. The major game changes are more about usability than strategy: price-change trends are now built into the site, price changes move to midnight UK time, gameweeks close at 9am the morning after the final match to allow late bonus and defensive-contribution updates, and ranks, mini-leagues and points update live. Transfers still roll up to a maximum of five and carry through wildcards and free hits, while the two sets of wildcard, free hit, bench boost and triple captain remain split across the first and second halves of the season.

The squad starts with David Raya in goal and a £4.0m backup, because Arsenal coverage still looks important despite a mixed opening run after Coventry at home in Gameweek 1 and possible defensive absences for William Saliba and uncertainty around Declan Rice. Gabriel is judged too expensive at £8.0m when Raya and cheaper defenders offer routes into the same clean sheets. The plan is to pair Raya with a cheaper Arsenal defender if one of Cristhian Mosquera, Piero Hincapie or Riccardo Calafiori looks nailed by Gameweek 1, while an Ezri Konsa move to Arsenal would be a potentially major value pick. Harry Maguire is included at £5.0m for Manchester United because of minutes, improved defensive form, attacking threat and defensive-contribution potential, while Nottingham Forest’s £4.5m Reyna Cunha appeals as a likely starting centre-back under Oliver Glasner. Coventry’s Thomas is a £4.0m bench defender with attacking returns and strong defensive-contribution numbers, and Malick Tchou is backed despite a tough opener against Liverpool because his fixtures improve from Gameweek 2 or 3.

In midfield, Bruno Fernandes is the premium pick thanks to Manchester United’s early fixtures, though there is an acknowledgement that cheaper alternatives such as Bryan, Ben Mee or Matheus Cunha could release funds if captaincy is not obvious in Gameweek 1. Rogers at £7.5m is selected for Chelsea, expected to play as one of the No.10s behind Joao Pedro alongside Cole Palmer; Palmer at £9.5m is admired but too expensive for this structure. Dominik Szoboszlai is preferred as a cheaper route after falling £0.5m short of Wirtz, with penalties, set pieces and talisman status part of the appeal. James Garner completes the playable midfield as an Everton value pick with kind opening fixtures and reliable defensive-contribution potential, while a £4.5m midfielder sits on the bench.

The formation is currently 3-4-3 after several forwards come in cheaper than expected. Haaland is locked in, Joao Pedro at £7.5m is viewed as excellent value with Chelsea’s favourable early run around one Arsenal fixture, and Calvert-Lewin takes the £6.0m forward slot for now. Igor Jesus at Nottingham Forest and Osula are live alternatives, with Igor Jesus particularly interesting after a strong pre-season. Chip strategy remains flexible: the wildcard is likely to be saved until the team needs it rather than forced around a clear fixture swing, the free hit is on the radar for Gameweek 4 when Manchester United play Manchester City and Chelsea host Hull, and Gameweek 12 is another possible free-hit spot if Arsenal v Manchester City clashes with heavy investment. Triple captain looks straightforwardly tied to Haaland’s home matches against promoted sides Coventry, Ipswich and Hull, while bench boost is more likely to wait until after a wildcard despite some early options in Gameweeks 1, 2 and 6.
key_points: First draft spends the full £100.0m and includes Haaland, Bruno Fernandes, Raya, Rogers, Szoboszlai, James Garner, Joao Pedro and Calvert-Lewin.
Gabriel is avoided at £8.0m; Arsenal defensive coverage comes through Raya plus a cheaper defender if Mosquera, Hincapie, Calafiori or a potential Konsa transfer looks nailed.
Harry Maguire is backed at £5.0m for minutes, set-piece threat and defensive-contribution potential, while Reyna Cunha is a £4.5m Nottingham Forest value defender under Glasner.
Joao Pedro at £7.5m and Rogers at £7.5m form a Chelsea double-up, helped by strong early fixtures including Fulham away in Gameweek 1, though the double-up is not considered essential.
The £6.0m forward slot is currently Calvert-Lewin, with Igor Jesus and Osula close alternatives; Igor Jesus is highlighted as a strong pre-season watchlist pick.
Wildcard strategy is patient, with no obvious early fixture swing forcing a Gameweek 6-style move at this stage.
Free hit options include Gameweek 4, when Manchester United face Manchester City and Chelsea host Hull, and Gameweek 12, when Arsenal play Manchester City.
Triple captain is likely to target Haaland at home to promoted sides, with Coventry, Ipswich and Hull all visiting Manchester City in the first half of the season.

items.title: MY FIRST FPL DRAFT 🔥 588 IN THE 🌏 LAST SEASON 🚀  FANTASY PREMIER LEAGUE 26/27 TIPS
items.headline: Haaland and Fernandes anchor a value-heavy first FPL draft for 2026/27
items.summary:
The first 2026/27 draft builds around Erling Haaland at £15.5m and Bruno Fernandes at £12.0m, with the lack of Mohamed Salah in the game making that premium pairing feel easier to accommodate. Haaland is viewed as close to essential because of captaincy: Manchester City open with Bournemouth at home, then have Coventry at home in Gameweek 3, Sunderland at home in Gameweek 5 and Ipswich at home in Gameweek 7. Fernandes also has immediate appeal with Hull away in Gameweek 1 and Ipswich at home in Gameweek 2, plus penalties, reliable minutes and a No 10 role, but he is not seen as enough reason to fade Haaland from the start.

The defence is deliberately restrained, with no player above £5.5m. Kinsky is the preferred £4.5m goalkeeper at Spurs, assuming he starts under De Zerbi after signs of defensive improvement late last season. Arsenal’s Mosquera is selected at £5.5m as a value route into their back line while Saliba is short of fitness, though his minutes could become a concern if Arsenal sign another centre-back or once availability improves. Gabriel at £8.0m is considered too expensive for now, while Calafiori is noted as an alternative £5.5m Arsenal pick with some left-back competition risk. Neco Williams, Joe Rodon, Antonee Robinson and Lewis Hall complete a cheap, rotation-friendly defensive group, with Robinson especially interesting if Fulham’s penalty situation leaves him in contention after Harry Wilson and Gimenez move on.

Midfield is currently a five-man setup. Cole Palmer is backed at £9.5m because he has penalties, strong minutes, a full summer off and fixtures good enough outside Arsenal away in Gameweek 3, though he is the likeliest downgrade if extra funds are needed, with Morgan Gibbs-White at £8.0m the obvious alternative. Dominik Szoboszlai is included at £7.0m as a potentially underpriced Liverpool starter, especially if he has any chance of penalties after Salah’s departure, though Isak is acknowledged as the likelier taker. Ismaila Sarr at £6.5m and Ndiaye at £6.0m round out the midfield as nailed, attacking options, with Ndiaye favoured over similarly priced alternatives because of penalties and stronger underlying numbers last season.

Up front, Haaland is paired with Dominic Calvert-Lewin, while a £4.5m non-playing forward sits on the bench in the current 3-5-2 structure. There is a clear possibility the next draft shifts to 3-4-3 because the £6.0m forward bracket looks unusually strong: Evanilson, Calvert-Lewin, Newcastle’s Osula if he starts at No 9, Igor Jesus under Glasner, and Dominic Solanke at Spurs all offer routes into better attacking upside if their fitness, roles and minutes look secure in pre-season. For now, the guiding principle is to prioritise nailed players after last season’s success came from avoiding unnecessary minutes risks, while leaving flexibility to redistribute money from Palmer or Fernandes before Gameweek 1 if the squad balance demands it.
key_points: Haaland is the central pick despite his £15.5m price, mainly because City face Bournemouth, Coventry, Sunderland and Ipswich at home in the first seven gameweeks.
Bruno Fernandes is strongly favoured for Hull away in Gameweek 1 and Ipswich at home in Gameweek 2, with penalties, fitness and minutes making him a safe premium midfielder.
Kinsky is the preferred £4.5m goalkeeper if he is confirmed as Spurs’ first choice; no playing £4.0m goalkeeper is currently identified.
Gabriel at £8.0m is swerved for now, with Mosquera at £5.5m offering a cheaper Arsenal route while Saliba is short of fitness, albeit with clear minutes risk.
Cole Palmer is backed at £9.5m but is the most obvious midfield downgrade to Morgan Gibbs-White if money is needed elsewhere.
Szoboszlai at £7.0m is a high-upside Liverpool pick if nailed, especially if pre-season suggests he has any penalty involvement after Salah’s exit.
A switch from 3-5-2 to 3-4-3 is very possible because £6.0m forwards such as Calvert-Lewin, Evanilson, Osula, Igor Jesus and Solanke all look viable if their roles are confirmed.
Robinson is a notable £4.5m bench defender because he may have some penalty potential for Fulham and rotates into kinder fixtures after Gameweek 1.

items.title: FPL IS LIVE! 🎉 | LIVE Q&A + ANALYSIS! | FPL Tips 26/27
items.summary: {"headline":"Mosquera discount is the early key to Haaland-Bruno drafts","summary":"The first pricing reaction centres on whether Erling Haaland at £15.5m, Bruno Fernandes at £12.0m and Gabriel at £8.0m can coexist. The answer is yes, but only with real compromises, and Gabriel is the likeliest premium to lose his place because Arsenal offer cheaper defensive routes. With William Saliba ruled out for a spell, Mosquera at £5.5m looks a strong early pick; if the rumoured Ezri Konsa move to Arsenal happens at £4.5m, he becomes close to template. Going without Gabriel carries obvious pain risk from set pieces, but the saving transforms the rest of the squad.\n\nA quick workable structure includes Kinsky in goal, Kerkez, Van Hecke and Mosquera in defence, Bruno Fernandes, Morgan Rogers and Dominik Szoboszlai in midfield, and a front three of Jean-Philippe Mateta, Joao Pedro and Haaland, with Jorrel Hato, Bobby Thomas, Jason Steele and Will Hughes on the bench. Szoboszlai at £7.0m

items.title: FPL IS LIVE - LAUNCH PARTY 🚨
items.summary:
{"headline":"Isak at £9m complicates the Haaland-Bruno first draft","summary":"FPL is live for 2026/27 and the first major takeaway is that budgets are tight. Erling Haaland at £15.5m is the most expensive player in game history, Bruno Fernandes rises to £12.0m after a record-breaking assist season, and Gabriel reaches £8.0m after passing 200 points. The working preference is still to start with Haaland and Bruno because they cover the two key captaincy slots: Haaland is the obvious Gameweek 1 captain at home to Bournemouth, while Bruno has a strong alternative route against newly promoted Hull and then Ipswich in Gameweek 2. Hull’s weak Championship underlying numbers make that opener particularly attractive for Manchester United assets, even if United’s European schedule adds some long-term fatigue risk.

Gabriel is the hardest sacrifice. Arsenal project well for early clean sheets, but £8.0m is a huge outlay for a defender who is rarely captainable, especially when Haaland and Bruno already stretch the squad. Arsenal’s defence also carries uncertainty, with William Saliba expected to miss months, Jurrien Timber carrying an issue, Ben White’s status unclear and potential centre-back signings such as John Stones or Ezri Konsa still being discussed. Mosquera at £5.5m could become the cheaper Arsenal route if he looks first choice, while Raya at £6.0m is also viable but expensive.

The scoring changes push the early draft towards midfield and attacking full-backs. Defensive contributions remain, bonus is adjusted to help full-backs, and the removal of negative bonus for being tackled should lift midfielders. That makes a 3-5-2 structure appealing. Morgan Rogers at Chelsea for £7.0m looks close to essential if he nails a role under Xabi Alonso, especially with Chelsea having no European football. Cole Palmer at £9.5m is also tempting after a rest and recovery period, while Justin Kluivert’s move from forward to midfielder at £7.5m is interesting but Bournemouth’s opening run of Man City, Everton, Newcastle, Brentford, Liverpool and Chelsea makes him less urgent.

The surprise price is Alexander Isak at £9.0m, which immediately challenges the earlier idea of using Igor Thiago at £8.0m as the second forward. Isak’s proven output, likely penalties and discounted price make him very attractive, but fitting him alongside Haaland, Bruno and possibly Palmer causes the squad to break. Phil Foden at £7.0m is

items.title: Ok… Who really is the goat? 🐐 🇵🇹 🇦🇷? #worldcup #ronaldo #messi
items.headline: Messi dominates GOAT debate as Ronaldo, Pelé and Ronaldinho split the chasing pack
items.summary:
Lionel Messi emerges as the clear favourite in a rapid-fire greatest-footballer-of-all-time debate, with repeated answers of “Messi” and “Leo Messi” outweighing the rest of the field. Several respondents frame the choice as obvious, with one calling him “the biggest one” and another saying there is “no doubt” he is the GOAT.

Cristiano Ronaldo remains the strongest challenger. Multiple voices back “Cristiano,” “CR7” or “the guy I watched,” and one supporter explicitly chooses Ronaldo over Messi. The Ronaldo vote is split slightly by references to Brazilian Ronaldo, with several respondents naming “R9,” “Ronaldo Nazario” or “Brazilian Ronaldo” as their all-time pick.

Brazilian legends receive significant support beyond R9. Pelé is named several times, while Ronaldinho is a recurring sentimental choice, praised by fans who clearly value flair and joy as much as longevity or records. Zico also appears, reinforcing the Brazilian thread running through the discussion.

A handful of outlier picks add colour rather than shifting the overall balance: Neymar, Hakimi, Robbie Keane, Sadio Mané, Maradona and even Harry Maguire are mentioned. But the overall pattern is unmistakable: Messi leads the crowd, Cristiano Ronaldo is the main rival, and the classic Brazilian icons form the next tier of the debate.
key_points: Messi is the most frequently named GOAT, with many respondents answering immediately and emphatically.
Cristiano Ronaldo is the leading alternative, including direct support for CR7 over Messi.
Brazilian Ronaldo receives separate backing under names such as R9, Ronaldo Nazario and Brazilian Ronaldo.
Pelé and Ronaldinho are the most prominent legends outside the Messi-Ronaldo debate.
Several choices reflect personal attachment or style preference, including Ronaldinho, Neymar and Sadio Mané.
Outlier answers include Hakimi, Robbie Keane, Zico, Maradona and Harry Maguire.

items.title: ENGLAND TEAM PREVIEW 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿 | ANDY + FPL CHAI! | FIFA World Cup 2026 Fantasy Tips
items.headline: Kane leads England fantasy plans, but Konsa and O'Riley offer the value route
items.summary:
England enter the World Cup under Thomas Tuchel with strong fantasy appeal, even if the opening fixture against Croatia is not as explosive as some rival nations' Matchday 1 match-ups. The expected route is Croatia, Ghana and Panama in the group, with England fancied to go deep into the tournament, possibly at least the semi-finals, although a likely tougher knockout path and a potential Brazil quarter-final temper expectations. Tuchel's tournament pedigree and Harry Kane's career-best Bayern Munich form are central to the optimism.

The likely defensive shape has Jordan Pickford in goal, Reece James at right-back, Ezri Konsa and Marc Guehi at centre-back, and O'Riley at left-back. Pickford is viewed as reliable but not necessarily the best fantasy route, while James carries attacking upside through crossing to Kane but remains a minutes and fitness risk. Konsa, at 4.8m and around 1% owned, stands out because the game awards a two-point scouting bonus to sub-5% owned players who score more than four points. Guehi has set-piece threat, especially with Declan Rice's delivery, but Konsa's lower price and ownership make him the more attractive punt if friendlies confirm his place.

O'Riley is the preferred England defender. At 4.7m, he is the cheapest expected starter in the back line and brings major attacking upside after a breakout Premier League season under Pep Guardiola. His role under Tuchel needs monitoring, but if England form a back three in possession and allow him to attack the box, his aerial threat and goal potential make him the standout defensive pick. England's clean-sheet odds are solid rather than elite, with the panel expecting around two shutouts from Croatia, Ghana and Panama, but there is a case for waiting until Matchday 2 because Germany and Spain have stronger clean-sheet prospects in the opener.

Midfield is more complicated. Declan Rice and Elliot Anderson are likely secure for starts and minutes, but the game's scoring leans heavily towards attacking output, making them less exciting unless Rice's set pieces deliver. Bukayo Saka is the safest starter among the advanced midfielders but costs 9.5m and may be protected around the 60-70 minute mark after an injury-hit season. Morgan Rogers is backed as a possible starter ahead of Jude Bellingham, though the battle is close and friendlies are crucial; Bellingham becomes interesting if Tuchel clearly trusts him, but rotation risk remains. Marcus Rashford could start on the left ahead of Anthony Gordon, yet his minutes also look vulnerable.

Kane is the clear top England pick at 10.5m and over 40% ownership. He is on penalties, plays through the centre of everything England do, benefits from shots-on-target scoring and is in Ballon d'Or-level form. The main debate is opportunity cost: Germany, Spain, France and Norway have forwards with easier Matchday 1 fixtures and far higher projected team goals, so Kane is not an automatic opener for every strategy. Holding him through Matchdays 1 and 2 is viable, but if England win their first two games, the Panama fixture could bring heavy rotation and even an Ollie Watkins start, making a Matchday 3 wildcard or transfer plan important.
key_points: Harry Kane is England's No. 1 fantasy pick: 10.5m, penalty taker, elite form and viable from Matchday 1, though not necessarily the best Matchday 1 captain over forwards from Germany, Spain, France or Norway.
Ezri Konsa is the best differential defender if he starts: 4.8m, around 1% owned and eligible for the two-point sub-5% scouting bonus if he scores more than four points.
O'Riley is the preferred England defensive asset at 4.7m because of his attacking and aerial upside, but his exact role under Tuchel needs checking in the friendlies.
Reece James offers high ceiling through crossing and attacking quality, but fitness and 60-minute risk make him a wait-and-see option rather than a lock.
England's fixtures are Croatia, Ghana and Panama; Croatia is the trickiest opener, while Ghana and especially Panama are the better attacking entry points.
Rice and Anderson look like the safest midfield minutes, but Rice's set pieces are the main reason to consider either because defensive midfielders are not heavily rewarded.
Saka is nailed but expensive at 9.5m and may be managed for minutes; Rogers, Bellingham, Rashford and Gordon are all more dependent on friendly line-ups and Tuchel's preferences.
If England win their first two matches, Matchday 3 rotation is a major risk, with Ollie Watkins a possible Panama punt if Kane is rested.

items.title: How to play World Cup Fantasy? - Differences compared to FPL! 🤔 #worldcup2026
items.headline: In-play substitutions make strong benches essential in World Cup Fantasy 2026
items.summary:
World Cup Fantasy 2026 launches with several major differences from Fantasy Premier League, and managers need to adjust quickly. The scoring system is broader than FPL: midfielders gain points for tackles and key passes, forwards are rewarded for shots on target, and players earn points for winning penalties even if the spot-kick is missed. Free-kick goals also carry an extra reward, while defenders and goalkeepers score more heavily for clean sheets but are punished more for conceding goals.

The game also replaces the familiar FPL chip landscape with a new set of tournament-specific tools. The Wildcard remains, but four additional chips change strategic planning: Maximum Captain automatically gives the armband to the highest-scoring player in the starting XI, 12th Man adds an extra free player to the squad, Qualification Booster rewards players who reach the next knockout round, and a mystery chip is still to be revealed.

The biggest strategic shift is in-play management. Unlike FPL, World Cup Fantasy allows managers to remove a player who has already played and bring in a bench player whose match has not yet started. That makes a strong bench far more important than in a standard FPL setup, because low scorers can be replaced across the matchday rather than locked in permanently.

Captaincy also becomes a “stick or twist” decision. If an early captain performs well, managers can keep the points; if the score disappoints, they can sacrifice those captain points and move the armband to a later player who has not yet played. Success in World Cup Fantasy 2026 therefore depends less on a fixed XI and more on planning match order, bench strength, captaincy ladders and chip timing around the tournament schedule.
key_points: Midfielders score for tackles and key passes, while forwards gain points for shots on target.
Winning a penalty earns points even if the penalty is not converted.
Defenders and goalkeepers are rewarded more for clean sheets but penalised more for goals conceded.
A two-point scouting bonus is available for selecting players under 5% ownership.
New chips include Maximum Captain, 12th Man, Qualification Booster and a still-unrevealed mystery chip, alongside the Wildcard.
In-play substitutions allow managers to replace poor starters with bench players who have not yet played.
Captaincy can be switched from an already-played captain to a later player, turning each matchday into a stick-or-twist decision.
Building a strong bench is essential because substitutes are active tactical options, not just injury cover.

items.title: SPAIN TEAM PREVIEW 🇪🇸 | ANDY + FPL GENERAL! | FIFA World Cup 2026 Fantasy Tips
items.headline: Oyarzabal emerges as Spain’s safest route into dream opening fixtures
items.summary:
Spain enter the 2026 World Cup as one of the tournament favourites, with expectations of at least a semi-final or final built around a young, high-upside squad led by Lamine Yamal, Pedri and a strong midfield core. The opening schedule is the major fantasy draw: Cape Verde in Matchday 1 on June 15, Saudi Arabia in Matchday 2 on June 21, then Uruguay in Matchday 3. The first two fixtures look tailor-made for attacking points, but Spain also carry one of the biggest rotation risks in the game because several key players are managing fitness issues and the squad has enough depth to win those early matches without overextending stars.

Mikel Oyarzabal is the standout Spain pick. At 8.1m, he looks likely to start up front, is expected to be on penalties, has chance-creation routes to points and can score direct free-kicks. With Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams both injury concerns for Matchday 1, Oyarzabal’s security improves further, and he is even preferred over some premium forwards such as Harry Kane, Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Kai Havertz for the opening round because Spain’s fixture against Cape Verde has greater haul potential. Kylian Mbappe remains the premium forward lock, but Oyarzabal is viewed as close to essential alongside him.

The midfield recommendation centres on Pedri at 8.1m. Although Fabian Ruiz is cheaper at 6.8m and currently low-owned enough to qualify for the scouting bonus if he stays under 5%, Pedri’s set pieces, open-play creativity and recent Barcelona form make him the preferred Spanish midfielder. Rodri is less attractive without defensive contribution scoring, and both he and Ruiz may have managed minutes because of recent injuries and season workload. Mikel Merino is also worth monitoring after scoring six goals in qualifying, but he is only just returning from injury, while Gavi and Dani Olmo add further competition and rotation uncertainty.

In defence, Marc Cucurella is the preferred pick despite his 18.5% ownership removing any scouting-bonus upside. He appears the most secure full-back, plays advanced for Spain and should benefit from matches where Spain dominate territory. Unai Simon is the first-choice goalkeeper rather than David Raya, but a Spanish defensive slot may be less appealing than an attacking one unless managers want clean-sheet coverage. Aymeric Laporte is worth watching if his ownership remains below 5%, while Pedro Porro would become highly attractive if friendlies suggest he starts over Marcos Llorente at right-back because of his attacking and set-piece threat.

The main caution is Lamine Yamal. Despite ownership near 47%, he is a risky Matchday 1 pick because Spain do not need to force him back against Cape Verde, and the fantasy deadline arrives four days before Spain play. A better route may be to avoid him initially and use the 12th Man chip on him in Matchday 2 if his fitness is clearer. Nico Williams carries similar risk, while Ferran Torres could be a high-upside differential captain only if reliable team news confirms he starts on the wing.
key_points: Top Spain pick: Mikel Oyarzabal at 8.1m, likely starter, penalty taker and strong captain option against Cape Verde in Matchday 1.
Pedri is preferred in midfield thanks to set pieces, creativity and goal threat, despite Fabian Ruiz offering cheaper under-5% scouting-bonus potential.
Avoid Lamine Yamal in initial drafts unless fitness news improves; he is a better Matchday 2 12th Man candidate than a risky Matchday 1 starter.
Nico Williams is also an injury/managed-minutes concern, while Ferran Torres and Dani Olmo become differential routes only if team news points to starts.
Marc Cucurella is the favoured defensive pick, with attacking positioning and strong clean-sheet prospects in the first two matches.
Unai Simon is Spain’s first-choice goalkeeper, not David Raya, though pairing him with a lower-owned earlier-playing keeper could be a safer strategy.
Watch friendlies for the right-back battle: Pedro Porro has big fantasy upside, but Marcos Llorente currently looks more likely to start.
Spain’s best fantasy window is Matchdays 1-2 against Cape Verde and Saudi Arabia; Matchday 3 against Uruguay brings tougher opposition and likely rotation.

items.title: HOW TO PLAY WORLD CUP FANTASY! | Interactive Guide, New Rules & Pro Tips for World Cup 2026 🏆
items.headline: World Cup 2026 Fantasy rewards active managers with manual subs, captain switches and differential bonuses
items.summary:
World Cup 2026 Fantasy gives managers a $100m budget to build a 15-man squad: two goalkeepers, five defenders, five midfielders and three forwards, with a maximum of three players per nation in the group stage. Only the starting XI scores unless a benched player auto-subs in for a non-playing starter, but the game strongly rewards building a full squad rather than just an elite XI because manual substitutions and captain switches can be made during each match day while fixtures are still unfolding.

Scoring differs sharply from FPL. Goalkeepers and defenders earn five points for a clean sheet but are punished heavily for conceding multiple goals, making weak defensive picks risky. Defenders get seven points for goals, midfielders get six and forwards get five, while midfielders gain extra points for tackles and chances created, and forwards gain extra points for shots on target. That boosts the appeal of creative midfielders such as Bruno Fernandes, Raphinha and Michael Olise, as well as high-volume shooters like Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Harry Kane. A major wrinkle is the scouting bonus: any player under 5% ownership at the deadline earns an extra two points if they score at least four points in that match, rewarding managers who find genuine differentials.

The tournament is split into eight match days, from the three group-stage rounds through the round of 32, round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals, and the final plus third-place playoff. Managers get unlimited transfers before Matchday 1 and again before Matchday 4 after the group stage, with two free transfers for Matchdays 2 and 3, then four before Matchdays 5 and 6, five before Matchday 7 and six before Matchday 8. Extra transfers cost three points each, prices do not change during the tournament, and nation limits rise as the field narrows: four players per country in Matchdays 4-5, five in the quarter-finals, six in the semi-finals and eight for Matchday 8.

The biggest edge comes from in-play management. The optimal structure is to start players whose teams play early in a match day and leave later-playing assets on the bench, allowing underperformers to be manually removed before the substitutes play. The same principle applies to captaincy: start with an early fixture, then twist to later options if the score is not good enough. A rough threshold is to stick on 12+ points early in a match day, around 10 in the middle, and eight or fewer near the end depending on remaining options. Once a manual sub is made, automatic substitutions are disabled, and once a captaincy or substitution decision is made away from a player who has already played, it cannot be reversed.

There are five boosters, with four confirmed: Maximum Captain automatically doubles the highest scorer in the squad for that match day; 12th Man adds one uncaptainable player with no budget or nation-limit restriction; Wild Card allows unlimited transfers in a non-unlimited-transfer round; and Qualification Booster adds two points to each starting XI player whose nation progresses, available only from Matchday 4 onward. The fifth booster remains a mystery until later in the tournament, so early planning should focus on using the known chips around fixture density, knockout progression and squad repair needs.
key_points: Build a strong 15-man squad, not just a starting XI, because manual substitutions let later-playing bench players replace poor early scorers.
Captain early-playing assets first, then switch if needed; sensible stick thresholds are roughly 12+ points early, 10 mid-matchday and around eight late on.
Creative midfielders benefit from chances created and tackles, making players like Bruno Fernandes, Raphinha and Michael Olise especially attractive under the scoring system.
High-volume forwards such as Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Harry Kane gain extra value from the shots-on-target scoring rule.
The scouting bonus gives two extra points to sub-5% owned players who score at least four points, making low-owned differentials unusually valuable.
Transfers are unlimited before Matchday 1 and Matchday 4; extra transfers beyond the free allocation cost three points, and player prices do not change.
Nation limits rise from three players per country in the group stage to four in Matchdays 4-5, five in Matchday 6, six in Matchday 7 and eight in Matchday 8.
Boosters include Maximum Captain, 12th Man, Wild Card and Qualification Booster, with the Qualification Booster only usable from the round of 32 onward.

items.title: HOW I FINISHED TOP 1,000 🔥 FPL 2025/26 SEASON REVIEW
items.headline: Bruno Fernandes captaincy seals a 588th-place FPL finish after late Gameweek 38 chaos
items.summary:
A 68-point Gameweek 38 delivers a final green arrow from 926th to 588th overall, securing a first top-1,000 Fantasy Premier League finish after 16 seasons. The decisive final-week swing comes with plenty of fortune: Erling Haaland to Jarrod Bowen goes through just before the deadline, but an attempted Bukayo Saka to Mohamed Salah move fails as the site struggles. That leaves Bruno Fernandes as captain instead of Salah, and his goal, assist and bonus points become a 28-point captaincy haul. Bowen adds 12 points, Antoine Semenyo scores, Trent Alexander-Arnold returns 10, and late defensive contribution points for James Hill help push the final rank inside the top 600.

The season review shows how central reliable, nailed players are to the success. Haaland is the most-captained player and dominates the first half of the campaign with repeated double-digit returns. Fernandes is the most-started player and becomes the defining pick of the season, scoring heavily from a role that combines strong underlying numbers, penalties, set pieces and defensive contributions. Arsenal defensive assets David Raya and Gabriel also prove crucial, with Raya finishing as the top-scoring goalkeeper and Gabriel combining clean sheets, goal threat, bonus and defensive contribution points.

The most-used squad core reflects a low-maintenance, reliability-first approach: Raya, Gabriel, Virgil van Dijk, Sessi, Anderson, Fernandes, Saka, Semenyo, Kiernan Dewsbury-Hall, Haaland and Thiago. Dewsbury-Hall works as a cheap nailed Everton number 10, Semenyo becomes a major asset across Bournemouth and Manchester City, and Thiago emerges as a strong value forward after arriving as Ivan Toney’s replacement. The defence is deliberately left largely untouched between the Gameweek 4 and Gameweek 32 wildcards, with most defensive transfers focused on Arsenal because of their elite clean-sheet potential.

The main lessons for 2026/27 are clear: accept luck as part of FPL, avoid tilting after bad outcomes, prioritise nailed minutes, and do not chase short-term form too aggressively. Defensive contribution points are now a major part of squad building, though their impact depends on next season’s prices; players like Elliot Anderson, Gabriel and Sessi are likely to rise. Rolling transfers remains underrated because banking two, three or more moves creates flexibility for structural changes without hits. There is also a case for FPL to improve forwards, with midfielders currently benefiting from extra goal points, clean-sheet points and more frequent defensive contribution upside.
key_points: Final rank is 588th overall after a 68-point Gameweek 38, rising from 926th and securing a first top-1,000 finish.
Gameweek 38 luck is decisive: Haaland to Bowen succeeds, Saka to Salah fails, and Bruno Fernandes remains captain for a 28-point haul.
Jarrod Bowen scores 12 points in the final week, while Trent Alexander-Arnold adds 10 and James Hill gains late defensive contribution points.
Bruno Fernandes is the season’s key asset, combining nailed minutes, penalties, set pieces, strong underlying numbers and defensive contributions.
David Raya and Gabriel are major long-term wins, with Arsenal’s defence trusted heavily and very few defender transfers made between wildcards.
Defensive contribution players are central to the strategy, but likely price rises for assets such as Elliot Anderson, Gabriel and Sessi may reduce their value next season.
Rolling transfers is viewed as underrated because multiple free moves allow bigger restructuring without hits; no hits are taken all season.
Bench Boost in Gameweek 1 is under consideration for 2026/27 to remove the chip early, while more early-season differential risk is also on the table.
